issues.title: Semantics produces illegible errors in non-debug builds when test fakes text input
issues.body:
When running an integration test that interacts with a TextField, multiple errors get thrown from the framework that cause the test to fail. Note that this only happens when not running with the default `--debug` flag (meaning running with `--release` or `--profile`) but we do not want to run our integration tests in `--debug` mode as we'd like the tests to accurately portray behavior in the "real" environment.

I first ran with `-d web-server` as instructed [here](https://flutter.dev/docs/cookbook/testing/integration/introduction#5b-web)

Terminal Command: `flutter drive --driver=int_test/util/flutter_driver.dart --target=int_test/my_int_test.dart -d web-server --profile --keep-app-running`
Terminal Output:
```
Running "flutter pub get" in flutter_int_test_on_web...            750ms
Launching int_test/my_int_test.dart on Web Server in profile mode...
Compiling int_test/my_int_test.dart for the Web...                 30.9s
int_test/my_int_test.dart is being served at http://localhost:63807
The web-server device requires the Dart Debug Chrome extension for debugging. Consider using the Chrome or Edge devices for an improved development workflow.
[INFO]: http://localhost:63807/ 64:26 "Installed new service worker."
[INFO]: http://localhost:63807/main.dart.js 15661:16 "00:00 +0: test example"
Failure Details:
Failure in method: test example

end of failure 1



Leaving the application running.
```
Chromium Console Output: Chromium never even opens

I noticed the error message "The web-server device requires the Dart Debug Chrome extension for debugging. Consider using the Chrome or Edge devices for an improved development workflow." even though I have downloaded the [Dart Debug Extension](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/dart-debug-extension/eljbmlghnomdjgdjmbdekegdkbabckhm). I then tried removing `-d web-server`.


Terminal Command: `flutter drive --driver=int_test/util/flutter_driver.dart --target=int_test/my_int_test.dart --profile --keep-app-running`

What is the reason for this? I do not see how it would be unuseful to be able to see all output in the terminal regardless of build type.

comments:
@JordanRoberts1 
Thanks for the report. For some reason, the github repo you shared isn't accessibile / opening for me, so I can't see the code.
Mean time, when you say `When running an integration test that interacts with a TextField`, I am assuming you are using `tester.enterText()` to input in the textfield, correct ?

If so, there's a [similar](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/89749) open issue related to `enterText` not working in release mode. Please check it and see if it sounds similar to your case.

Thanks @JordanRoberts1 for the updated repo. I cloned and ran it in profile mode and do see a bunch of exceptions, although, it does seem to be related to `enterText()` method, because if you comment out `await tester.enterText(find.byType(TextField), 'hello');` and subsequent `expect(find.text('hello'), findsOneWidget);`, and run the test in profile mode, you'll see the test passes, as below:

```
Launching int_test/my_int_test.dart on Chrome in profile mode...
Compiling int_test/my_int_test.dart for the Web...                 28.1s
[INFO]: http://localhost:50938/ 64:26 "Installed new service worker."
[INFO]: http://localhost:50938/main.dart.js 15528:16 "00:00 +0: test example"
[INFO]: http://localhost:50938/main.dart.js 15528:16 "00:01 +1: (tearDownAll)"
[INFO]: http://localhost:50938/main.dart.js 15528:16 "00:01 +2: All tests passed!"
All tests passed.
Leaving the application running.
dhs@Dhss-MacBook-Pro flutter_int_test_on_web % 
```

Hence I am inclined towards stating that the root-cause is `enterText()` method, for which, https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/89749 is already present to address it. You may share your findings in that issue, so that it'll help us for better tracking.

@darshankawar thank you for validating the issue. I have cross linked this issue in the comments section of https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/89749. Please let me know if there is any help I can provide to resolving this issue.

I am keeping it open since there are bunch of exceptions shown in the log to help diagnose the issue.
Similar / related : https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/89749

The lack of output in profile and release builds is filed in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/69070.

The remaining issue is why `TextField` crashes in profile/release mode. We have an integration test that exercises text fields, but it doesn't use `tester.testTextInput` and it sets `semanticsEnabled: false`. I think the problem is that the test it trying to fake text input (`tester.testTextInput` is fake) but it uses real semantics. So when the semantics attempts to update the engine-side text field it's not finding anything. Since profile and release modes run without assertions the code keeps on trucking until it crashes in a place that shouldn't have been reached by the test.

I see two options:

- Disable semantics
- Don't fake text input

On the Flutter side we could either produce a better error message in non-debug builds, or fake text input in semantics when we know that the test is faking text input.

issues.title: Problems with Autocomplete and OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder in 3.32
issues.body:
Relates to [#165249](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/165249) and [#164034](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/164034)
In version 3.32, Autocomplete uses the new OverlayPortal API - overlayChildLayoutBuilder. It uses information about coordinate transformation from the child to the Overlay widget during the layout phase.
Previously, there were CompositedTransformFollower and CompositedTransformTarget widgets that used coordinate transformation during the compositing phase. As a result, changes have occurred in the work of the Autocomplete widget if it is a descendant of those widgets that calculate transformations after the layout phase (during the paint and compositing phases). At least, these are CompositedTransformFollower (using the compositing phase) and Flow (using the paint phase). 
And if, when using the first one, the assertion "The paint transform cannot be reliably computed because of RenderFollowerLayer(s)" is thrown, then nothing will happen with Flow.
I think @LongCatIsLooong just forgot about the Flow widget. But throwing an assertion means that we are not able to use any widgets based on OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder in CompositedTransformFollower and Flow at all. It can break many projects that use Autocomplete as a descendant of CompositedTransformFollower.
And although it would not be possible to calculate the correct coordinate transformation during the layout phase, this is often not critical. When animating, we get a one-frame lag when using the CompositedTransformFollower and Flow widgets.
But knowing the animation function (which is usually the case), it is possible to calculate the addition to the transformation so that such lag is no longer visible (or decrease).

Therefore, this assertion should either be removed by specifying in the documentation that the CompositedTransformFollower and Flow widgets will have a one-frame lag during animations, or allow developers to change this behavior. For example, SnapshotWidget - the mode parameter. 
If there is a descendant that cannot be rasterized, it has 3 modes: an assertion will be thrown, this descendant will not be displayed in the image, the original render object will be used instead of the image.
But I think removing the assertion would be better and much simpler.


I have created examples in dartpad that show these issues.
Each of them has a DropdownMenu where one can choose how to wrap the widget - without a wrapper (Simple), in Flow (Flow) or in CompositedTransformFollower (Follower). I tested locally by removing the lines of code in OverlayPortal that cause this assertion, and in this case the Follower mode works identically to the Flow mode - the same one-frame lag.

Here are three dartpad links:
The first one uses pure OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder.
It only works in the main and beta versions (there is no overlayChildLayoutBuilder in stable yet).
To enable the correction, turn on the Switch. Then it should be visible that the lag disappears (or decreases) in the Flow mode. In the Follower mode the assertion will be thrown.
Link:
[https://dartpad.dev/?id=efe9127f451bc28a285b83549c78abcd&channel=main](https://dartpad.dev/?id=efe9127f451bc28a285b83549c78abcd&channel=main)

The second one shows Autocomplete in stable channel with version 3.29.
Here, all three modes are identical, with no one-frame delays or exceptions.
[https://dartpad.dev/?id=293c8d2b25a7074f6ccf8fffe3a471e2&channel=stable](https://dartpad.dev/?id=293c8d2b25a7074f6ccf8fffe3a471e2&channel=stable)

The third one shows Autocomplete in the main channel with version 3.32.
Simple mode - no lags, Flow mode - one-frame lags with the correction possibility (via Switch), Follower mode - the assertion will be thrown after clicking on Autocomplete.
The correction widget walks through the element tree, finding the element for OverlayPortal widget inside the Autocomplete widget and updating its widget using the "update" method.
Updates it to redefine its builder by changing its Transform widget.
It is a hack, but this approach allows reducing the one-frame lag problem, sometimes quite significantly.
The correction may not work well on weak devices. Therefore, it is better to test on a desktop. But in the release mode on my Android device, it also worked well.
This is one of the solutions to the above problem that can be used and will only work if no assertion is thrown.
[https://dartpad.dev/?id=ef5fe192d8e79118111b3f6daf79ff93&channel=main](https://dartpad.dev/?id=ef5fe192d8e79118111b3f6daf79ff93&channel=main)

In summary, I think that removing the FlutterError assertion ("The paint transform cannot be reliably computed because of RenderFollowerLayer(s)") and writing a note in the OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder documentaion would be the best way, because it seems that other solutions may lead to new problems. And developers will be able to make their own decisions about it.

These videos show one frame lag and correction for the Flow and CompositedTransformFollower widgets (tested on my local Windows machine):

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cb9259bb-93d4-4aec-85f4-419cf1ddd59e

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/440f6ba1-cf6c-47ce-9d3e-e910d08becf6
comments: Keeping the issue open and labeling based on the issue description, for team's tracking.

@itspectre Can you confirm that you tried this on master? Just making sure since this code was changing recently.

CC @victorsanni from triage, any idea if this is a problem with the framework or with the code here?
The `triaged-framework` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-framework` label or `fyi-framework` label.

@justinmc @Piinks 
I have created this issue when the stable version was 3.29, and main/beta - 3.32.
Currently, the stable branch can be used:
[https://dartpad.dev/?id=efe9127f451bc28a285b83549c78abcd&channel=stable](https://dartpad.dev/?id=efe9127f451bc28a285b83549c78abcd&channel=stable)

[https://dartpad.dev/?id=ef5fe192d8e79118111b3f6daf79ff93&channel=stable](https://dartpad.dev/?id=ef5fe192d8e79118111b3f6daf79ff93&channel=stable)

I changed the code a bit a while ago, but it was due to some optimization and simplification.
The original problem has not changed. And it has nothing to do with text input ("team-text-input" tag).
I would try to explain it again in short words.
In [#164034](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/164034) the new overlayChildLayoutBuilder method was introduced for the OverlayPortal widget.
Its key feature is getting the transform matrix from the current RenderObject to Overlay's render object during the layout phase.
Therefore, it cannot work correctly if there is a widget between it and the parent Overlay that calculates the transformation matrix after the layout phase.
In [#165249](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/165249) the Autocomplete widget has been changed for using overlayChildLayoutBuilder instead of CompositedTransformFollower/CompositedTransformTarget.
As a consequence, the Autocomplete widget also has such limitations.

From the documentation:
**_The overlayChildBuilder callback is called during layout. 
To ensure the paint transform of OverlayPortal.child in relation to the target Overlay is up-to-date by then, all RenderObjects between the OverlayPortal to the target Overlay must establish their paint transform during the layout phase, which most RenderObjects do.
One exception is the CompositedTransformFollower widget, whose RenderObject only establishes the paint transform when composited. Putting a CompositedTransformFollower between the OverlayPortal and the Overlay may resulting in an incorrect child paint transform being provided to the overlayChildBuilder and will cause an assertion in debug mode._**
[https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/OverlayPortal/OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder.html](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/OverlayPortal/OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder.html)

About **"One exception is the CompositedTransformFollower widget"** - it is not true, because there is the Flow widget, at least, which also establishes its children paint transform after the layout phase (during the paint phase).
[https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Flow-class.html](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Flow-class.html)
_**Rather than positioning the children during layout, the children are positioned using transformation matrices during the paint phase using the matrices from the [FlowDelegate.paintChildren](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/rendering/FlowDelegate/paintChildren.html) function. The children can be repositioned efficiently by only repainting the flow, which happens without the children being laid out again (contrast this with a [Stack](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Stack-class.html), which does the sizing and positioning together during layout).**_

Developers can also create their own similar widgets.
Therefore, it looks very inappropriate to cause an asssertion only for the CompositedTransformFollower widget. But if this assertion will be thrown also for the Flow widget (and maybe some others), it creates really many problems. For example, the Flow widget
can be used for creating effects like "staggered menu animation" ([https://docs.flutter.dev/cookbook/effects/staggered-menu-animation](https://docs.flutter.dev/cookbook/effects/staggered-menu-animation)). And it will be impossible to use an Autocomplete widget in such menu.
Additionally, it will create the same problem for all other future widgets that will be based on that new overlayChildLayoutBuilder method.
Often, there isn't a big problem with the transformation matrix being incorrect. Especially if there is only a short animation. But even with a long animation there are some methods that can fix that one-frame lag. I showed one of them above. It uses an estimate of the transformation in a future frame. But I also have found a better solution, which will allow one to accurately adjust the transformation. I'll write it a bit later.
All such solutions can be included in third-party packages.
But that assertion can break them.
In the links above, I have shown 3 situations: a normal situation (without Flow and CompositedTransformFollower) - no lags, Flow - there is one-frame lag that can be fixed using the presented method and CompositedTransformFollower - there is the assertion (but by removing the assertion locally I observed a situation similar to the Flow widget variant).

Sorry for the breakage! Yeah I definitely wasn't aware that Flow is positioning its children during the paint phase, thanks for pointing that out.

Regarding the autocomplete change, my plan was to test the new API out in the autocomplete widget and see anything breaks, and I'm hoping that it can eventually replace  `CompositedTransformFollower` in most use cases, since it allows you to resize the child based on its location in the Overlay and doesn't have the paint order limitation. 

The "can't have `CompositedTransformFollower` in the path" limitation is a tricky problem that I don't have answers for (without significantly changing the layout / paint flow of the framework's rendering layer). However `Flow` repositioning children during the paint phase instead of the layout phase shouldn't make it impossible to use with `overlayChildLayoutBuilder` since `RenderTransform` does that too. The only requirement (IIRC) is that the `applyPaintTransform` implementation of the render object must be able to give the correct paint transform after layout is called which I think Flow should be able to do (but probably not with the current API? Since the delegate's paint method has side effects so we can't use it to query the child transforms).

The autocomplete implementation needs overlayChildLayoutBuilder, otherwise the options view will have to do layout with outdated constraints which is a dangerous (the framework will assert if the resulting size is larger the the current constraints permitted, for example) thing to do, and some features will become difficult / impossible to implement. So I still think Autocomplete should use the `overlayChildLayoutBuilder` API otherwise there are bigger problems than not being able to use Autocomplete in a `CompositedTransformFollower`.

Regarding removing the assert:

The framework fails silently when there's a `CompositedTransformFollower` will probably be confusing. Developers will be surprised if the paint transform is incorrect (especially in tests). We could add a debug flag to turn the assert off but without seeing a real world example where the new API has to be used in a `CompositedTransformFollower` I don't know how useful the flag will be.

@LongCatIsLooong 
_"However Flow repositioning children during the paint phase instead of the layout phase shouldn't make it impossible to use with overlayChildLayoutBuilder since RenderTransform does that too."_
`RenderTransform` gets a new transform matrix during the build phase from a new widget, but `Flow` can pass the build phase. Therefore the problem with `Flow` seems not less than with `CompositedTransformFollower`. 
And it is not just Autocomplete problem, this will affect all future widgets based on the new API.
Moreover, any developer can create a package with a set of widgets, and some of them can calculate the transformation matrix after the layout phase. So the problem can occur even without Flow/CompositedTransformFollower and without throwing assertion. It is not often case, but it could be possible.

_"We could add a debug flag to turn the assert off"_ 
This flag should be global (probably in the `WidgetsBinding`), because turning it off everywhere is inconvenient, especially, that some widgets can include OverlayPortal inside themselves. But, as I said before, this won't help for similar widgets from third-party libraries.

There are some similar specific problems in the Flow widget:
<<_**The [Flow](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Flow-class.html) widget recomputes its children's positions (as used by hit testing) during the paint phase rather than during the layout phase.
Widgets like [Opacity](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Opacity-class.html) avoid painting their children when those children would be invisible due to their opacity being zero.
Unfortunately, this means that hiding a [Flow](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Flow-class.html) widget using an [Opacity](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Opacity-class.html) widget will cause bugs when the user attempts to interact with the hidden region, for example, by tapping it or clicking it.
Such bugs will manifest either as out-of-date geometry (taps going to different widgets than might be expected by the currently-specified [FlowDelegate](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/rendering/FlowDelegate-class.html)s), or exceptions (e.g. if the last time the [Flow](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Flow-class.html) was painted, a different set of children was specified).
To avoid this, when hiding a [Flow](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Flow-class.html) widget with an [Opacity](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Opacity-class.html) widget (or [AnimatedOpacity](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/AnimatedOpacity-class.html) or similar), it is wise to also disable hit testing on the widget by using [IgnorePointer](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/IgnorePointer-class.html). This is generally good advice anyway as hit-testing invisible widgets is often confusing for the user.**_>>

And it's just documented. But for `overlayChildLayoutBuilder`, every widget using this API should also have a note in its documentation about such problem.

I agree that working with widgets in `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` would be much easy, but this method has additional limitations and pitfalls. I'm also not saying that the Autocomlete widget should abandon using the new API and return to the `CompositedTransformFollower`. The new API easily solved the problems with the size of the menu options in the `Autocomplete` widget.
But I'm not sure if replacing  `CompositedTransformFollower` everywhere would be a good way. Especially for the context menu in the `EditableText`. But for some widgets this would be a good solution (maybe `RawMenuAnchor`).

"_The "can't have CompositedTransformFollower in the path" limitation is a tricky problem that I don't have answers for (without significantly changing the layout / paint flow of the framework's rendering layer)._"
As i wrote in the previous messages, there are some (more/less) decisions for this problem. In my links I showed one possible way. The first link shows the correction for pure `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` , the second - fix for the Autocomplete widget - i inject my special widget in OverlayPortal inside Autocomplete via walking through element tree, adding the needed transformation. I can correct one-frame lag for the Flow widget, but the CompositedTransformFollower widget throws assertion. If I remove the assertion from my local version of Flutter, I can also fix this lag, just like for the Flow widget. Threfore the asserions is  the main problem on this side.
Also, as I mentioned in my previous message, there is (probably) a solution for fixing the overlayChildLayoutBuilder problem (perhaps with some not important restrictions) without transform estimation for the next frame, but I need to test it more and create a good example. I'll write it here in a few days.

> there is (probably) a solution for fixing the overlayChildLayoutBuilder problem (perhaps with some not important restrictions) without transform estimation for the next frame, but I need to test it more and create a good example. I'll write it here in a few days.

Hi @itspectre, were you able to find a solution here?

Hi, @victorsanni!
In general, I can say that I found what I wanted (sorry for not providing examples earlier, but I wanted to research this issue more thoroughly).
But there is an important note here - this is the solution for a developer who has the one-frame delay problem in some widget subtree and wants to fix it.
It is not good variant for using by default everywhere. It is posible, but in this case it be more preferably (because of performance) way to replace `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` with `CompositedTransformFollower` or another compositing widget.
The key idea - we need to create a custom widget-wrapper with a custom compositing layer (that inherited from `ContainerLayer`/`OffsetLayer`/`TransformLayer`). Using this widget we can calculate needed additional transformation during the compositing phase. Generally speaking, we need to get the layout phase transformation matrix "L", and during the compositing phase in the `addToScene` method in our custom layer we extract the transformation matrix from the ancestor render object, using it as a real transformation "C", so the needed correction transformation would be C * L^(-1).
This will work because at the moment of calling the `addToScene` method of our custom layer all ancestor layers called their `addToScene` methods early and defined the needed transformations (for example, `CompositedTransformFollower` is among ancestor widgets). The render object for the `CompositedTransformFollower` is the `RenderFollowerLayer`, that gets its transformation matrix from its layer - `FollowerLayer`.
Therefore, we can get that transformation from the render tree. 
Also using the `Flow` widget, it calculates its children transformation during the painting phase, therefore during the compositing phase these render objects also have needed transformation.

I have created a very simple example to represent such a case:

[Dartpad: the simple example](https://dartpad.dev/?id=f0531b7e8e89c6e50ea247a04be69c67)

<video src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/988366d4-5372-45ba-8259-150f439b5fd4" controls></video>

Here it is possible to see an effect of such correction by switching.

If it is nessecary to correct the delay in an existing widget like `Tooltip` or `Autocomple`, where `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` is hidden inside them, we can walk through the element tree
for finding `OverlayPortal`'s elements and update it's widget with needed attributes using the `update` method and `buildScope`/`lockState` in the `BuildOwner`.
There are some nuances regarding this, for example, if there is a `LayoutBuilder` or someone needs to monitor the rebuilding of elements so that the corrector widget does not disappear, but this is generally solvable, although there may be rare problems.
For usually cases where a developer knows where there is one-frame delay problem and which widget subtree he/she needs to wrap (for example, subtree with `IconButton` or `Autocomplete`) it should works well.
If that assertion could be supressable, I'm going to make a package for this delay problem in the future.

Also it is possible to change some other options (not only transformations) during the compositing phase - colors, clipping, ImageFilter, etc.
There is another example - it is my older experiments with different options (including `Autocomplete` correction in the composing phase):

[Dartpad: more advanced experiments](https://dartpad.dev/?id=e234f4f8cf93c987752362a2d0189c8a)

<video src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/878fed58-2a1f-4e74-bfa4-7d63e55df4e0" controls></video>

All of the above applies to the point of view of a third-party developer. There may be another way - adding a similar mechanism at the framework level, for example, in `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder`.
It is possible to check transformation matrix by comparing it from the layout phase and in the `addPostFrameCallback` in the debug mode. If they are not equal (using some matrix norm) - a warning for a developer to turn on the compositing correction if this feature would be added to the `OverlayPortal` widget.

I would also like to show the examples of `Autocomplete` that the intended benefits of `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` can be achieved with `CompositedTransformFollower`.
I took the old implementation of `Autocomplete` with `CompositedTransformFollower`, but fixed the issue of menu options width, as it was in the previous version, and added some features.

One of the arguments for creating the `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` was the ability to change the position of the menu so that it appears where there is enough space.
In my implementation, this is done during the composition phase:

[Dartpad: autocomplete - 1](https://dartpad.dev/?id=33e105ee3142268855a1e3528e0ee61e)

<video src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4765b135-a7b3-4397-9b98-2bf317f4823f" controls></video>

Another argument in favor of `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` was the ability to create different widgets depending on the position of the target.
And although it is potentially possible to create an infinite number of widget subtree variants for `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder`,
in the case of using the composition phase there is also a way to achieve widget subtree switching (also with the old `Autocomplete` implementation):

[Dartpad: autocomplete - 2](https://dartpad.dev/?id=f8b17d9b6a41f834b63289d696d9caf1)

<video src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9739f8b2-aa09-48f9-81af-f29879058d6d" controls></video>

The idea is to create each widget subtree in its own compositing layer and select only one in their parent 'addToScene' method, as well as modify the corresponding render object for hit testing, semantics, focus, etc.

Updating the size of the follower widget in the last two examples  is done by constantly calling the `performLayout` method using relayout boundary to avoid unnecessary calls - 
the same mechanic that is used in `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder`. Therefore, in this case, the choice of `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` seems justified.


Above examples are just experiments, and they may require some refinement for production. I usually tested on Windows and the desktop web, so some features specific to mobile devices (for example, devicePixelRatio) may not be taken into account.

----

Returning to our issues (mine and @Sebastien-VZN), the main problem here is the assertion, and there are two points:
1) The equality of the matrices is not checked, the check is only for the type of RenderFollowerLayer among the ancestors, which makes such a check useless for other widgets and hinders in case there are no problems calculating the transformation.
Therefore, it should be changed to compare the matrices obtained during layout and at the end of the frame.
2) The current implementation actually forces developers to replace `CompositedTransformFollower` in all their code, even if they don't have a problem calculating the transformation or it will be a lesser problem, than changing a lot of code. Therefore, it should be easy way to disable the assertion. 
If a developer doesn't see any problems with `CompositedTransformFollower`, why does he/she need to change it everywhere for no reason?
In addition, using `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` seems more difficult due to the need to operate with matrix transformations at least at the simplest level.
The API in `CompositedTransformFollower` looks simpler - one just needs to define the anchor point for the target widget, the anchor point of the follower widget, and the offset.

If the Flutter team/contributors (especially @LongCatIsLooong) will agree with my suggestions for changes for that assertion, I can try to do a PR, it should be small.
It is also necessary to correct the documentation and add examples there for `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder`.

Regarding the choice between `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder` and `CompositedTransformFollower`, I offer a compromise way - **for those follower widgets whose size does not depend on the size of the target widget (`Tooltip`/ContextMenu in `EditableText`/`SelectableRegion` and so on), use `CompositedTransformTarget`/`CompositedTransformFollower`. In the case when the size of the follower widget depends on the size of the target widget (`DropdownMenu`, `RawMenuAnchor`, `Autocomplete`,  etc), use `OverlayPortal.overlayChildLayoutBuilder`** - here it makes sense - updating the follower widget size correctly.

issues.title: When popping a route, controls should reacquire focus without opening the keyboard or scrolling to make visible.
comments: CC @gspencergoog

@gspencergoog Please run the code on device or emulator where the in-build keyboard(virtual keyboard) is enabled. By default keyboard  is disable in IOS Simulator.. I am getting this issue in Android Device and Emulator.  I am not sure about IOS.

And yes we need to get previously focused widget when we return to a route. Showing cursor in the previously focused `TextField` is good. But **the main problem is keyboard is popping up**.

On going to next page, I am dismissing keyboard by pressing back button(the cursor is still blinking on `TextField`). So why when we return to a route the keyboard state is not persisted. And in my example I am scrolling down to get to the next button. Why the scroll state is not persisted.

If it is still "as designed",
In Native Android this is not the case(not sure about swift). Then why flutter is different.

The scroll state is persisted, but when you go back, it restores the focus to the text field, which causes it to scroll into view.  Notice that if you unfocus the text field before pushing (as in my example), then when you go back, the scroll state is unchanged.

If I understand you correctly, what you want is (only when popping a route) focus the text field without bringing up a keyboard, and without scrolling it into view.

Can you give a concrete example of an Android app doing this?  I mean, it's probably possible to find, but I've tried a few apps now, and none of the do what you describe: they all either bring up the keyboard again when I go back to the page that had a text field focused, even if I manually put away the keyboard, or the control is unfocused when I go back to them. See the gif below:

issues.title: Flutter throws an exception when typing something while holding selection endpoint
issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce

1. Select any text
2. Type any symbol while holding selection endpoint with finger (touch location should be outside the text)

**Expected results:** Nothing special happens.

**Actual results:** Application throws exception.

<details>
<summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({Key? key}) : super(key: key);

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      home: Material(
        child: Padding(
          padding: EdgeInsets.all(50.0),
          child: TextField(),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

</details>

I can reproduce this on `android`, but it doesn't seem to reproduce on `iOS` with the same steps I used to reproduce on `android`. The exception doesn't stop the app from working, and it probably isn't noticeable if you're using `flutter run`.

### Steps to reproduce
- Run the code sample below with the debugger attached (the app will pause for exceptions, that's when you'll see it)
- Enter some text into the `TextField` 
- Select the text and hold the text selection handle
- Enter some text while holding the text selection handle
- You might need multiple tries before it reproduces

In the video below, you see at a certain point, I'm typing but there's no text, that is because the debugger catches the exception and the app is paused. After resuming the app, that's when the text is captured in the textfield.

      home: Material(
        child: Padding(

    );
  }
}

> ```dart
>     final textPainter = TextPainter(
>       text: TextSpan(text: text),
>       textDirection: TextDirection.ltr,
>       maxLines: null,
>     );
> 
>     textPainter.layout(maxWidth: MediaQuery.of(context).size.width);
> ```
> 
> is probably not a reliable way to measure the text layout of a `TextField`. `TextField`s apply additional styles (from themes for example since it's a material widget) that may change the text layout drastically. For example the above text painter will use a font size of 14 by default but the material default is probably larger (and iirc it even depends on the locale).

Even if you manually set the text size to 14 and specify a static font we have issues. The paragraph width and the line width are not matching the actual painting width, so the line metrics are coming out wrong.
I could have sworn I was getting different results on this before and now it's back to misbehaving. Reopening while I look at it again.
Argh, this method is not actually getting the style from the text the way I thought it was.

Ok.

The framework does not appear to have a way to tell you the `TextStyle` used in a `TextField`.

I think it would be ideal if the `TextEditingController` had some way to access this. Perhaps some method parallel to `buildTextSpan` that did not take a `TextStyle` argument but instead used the text style of the widget it's controlling.

/cc @justinmc @LongCatIsLooong who probably have good ideas about this.
(My original confusion probably related to prints I added to the framework that looked right - I can confirm that if you use the same exact text style in both places things work out).
The `triaged-engine` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-engine` label or `fyi-engine` label.

I'm currently trying to expose more text layout metrics, the end goal is to expose an immutable interface for querying the current text layout, and allowing developers to listen for text layout changes. I'm planning to add that to the text related render objects but since there doesn't seem to be an easy way to get the render objects from `Text` or `EditableText` I guess adding a callback to these widgets would be the easiest way to expose them? E.g., `Text.onTextLayoutChanged(TextLayout newLayout)`, `EditableText.onTextLayoutChanged(TextLayout newLayout, Offset newOffset)`. But the catch is that it's not allowed to call `setState` synchronously in these callbacks.

A `TextEditingController` can be assigned to multiple text fields that use different paragraph styles and have completely different text layout so it's probably not the best place to do that.

> A `TextEditingController` can be assigned to multiple text fields that use different paragraph styles and have completely different text layout so it's probably not the best place to do that.

What happens to the value of a TextEditingController in this case?

Or is it that `TextEditingValue`s can have multiple styles associated with them?

2 `TextField`s can share the same `TextEditingController`. These 2 `TextField`s will share the same editing states (text, selection, composing region), but the `TextStyle`s and `ParagraphStyle` used to render the text can be different since the `Theme` and the widget configurations they apply may be different.

I can get the lines from the `Text` widget by following [this](https://stackoverflow.com/a/78341140/17798537). But this solution does not work for `TextField`. I think it will work if we know the default `style` of the `TextField` when we set it to null. At this moment we need to use the same style to make it work.
It will be better if the `Text` and `TextField` widgets have the `.onTextLineChange` function.

I'm taking a look at this and here's what I've found with @jmandawg's code sample:
- Main issue is that the engine never receives a semantic tree update that includes the text field. Because of this, we never create the associated input node, which leads to the broken text field in the app
- The semantic tree is generated on the framework side and the update is sent to the engine to make the appropriate DOM changes.  But the framework just doesn't generate the Textfield's semantic node. 
- The textfield gets ignored in the case where Navigator is rendered after the TextField, but works fine the other way around (as @desmond206x pointed out). 

@desmond206x, thank you for the additional details! However, on the latest master, I can't reproduce the broken textfield using your code sample.  On page load, the text field autofocuses and I'm able to type in it.  Inspecting the tree also shows that the input node is getting created. 

There is definitely an issue with the TextField + Navigator combo - there might also be one related to `InputDecoration`, but I haven't been able to reproduce this.  @1jasonb-br or @desmond206x are those issues still occurring on the latest version of master?

issues.title: [Android 12] Textfield `onchanged` callback triggered twice on emulator
issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run this code on Android Emulator:
```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(const MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({Key? key}) : super(key: key);

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
        home: Scaffold(
      body: Center(
        child: TextField(
          onChanged: callback,
        ),
      ),
    ));
  }

  void callback(String s) {
    print(s);
  }
}
```
2. Start typing in the textfield.

**Expected results:** <!-- what did you want to see? -->

The callback method should be called only once on value change.

**Actual results:** <!-- what did you see? -->

The callback method is called twice when typing from the keyboard and not the soft keyboard.

issues.title: Let iOS keyboard dismissal synchronize with scroll
issues.body:
Since https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/21814, keyboards will dismiss during scroll. For higher fidelity, rather than one single brisk dismissal event, the keyboard dismissal should ideally be done gradually along with the scroll gesture. 

Android counterpart: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/62876
comments: The upstream visual symptom to fix is https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/19279
I still need this.
@mleonhard This may help; https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_interactive_keyboard

Dear Google Flutter Team,
I am preparing an app for release on Android & iOS.  It has a small chat function.  I am struggling to get the UX to match expectations for iOS users.

A common use-case for chat functions is to scroll up and consult a previous message while composing a new message.  Users expect this on all platforms.  Flutter currently makes this very hard to implement with acceptable UX on iOS:
- A Flutter app using the `onDrag` mode will hide the keyboard as soon as the user begins scrolling.  This is jarring to iOS users who expect the keyboard to stay put.  
   - If the app displays messages with a `ListView` with `reverse: true`, when the keyboard hides, the list jumps down.  This is jarring, too.  The user scrolls the desired information into view and taps the text box.  The keyboard re-appears and the list jumps upward again, hiding the information they wanted to see.  The user must learn to scroll the information to the bottom edge of the screen before tapping the text box.  If they don't use the app frequently, they will forgot to do this and feel frustrated every time they use the Flutter app.
   - If the app displays messages with a non-reversed `ListView`, the app must scroll the list up to the proper position when the keyboard appears.  Figuring out how to do this is incredibly involved and may be impossible.  Also, when the user begins scrolling and the keyboard disappears, they are left with the list scrolled half-way up the screen.  They must move their finger down half the screen to continue scrolling.  Additionally, when the user is scrolling and a new message arrives, the app tries to scroll to the bottom.  This cancels their current scroll gesture.  This is jarring.  Therefore the non-reversed `ListView` requires large amounts of code to work around Flutter's poor behavior when scrolling and showing/hiding the keyboard.
- A Flutter app could configure the keyboard with a `done` button by passing [`TextInputAction`](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/TextInputAction-class.html).  This means the app cannot use the `newline` or `send` buttons which are much more useful.
- The third-party module [`flutter_interactive_keyboard `](https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_interactive_keyboard) provides the function requested in this issue.  It is at v0.2.0.  It has no tests.  Every team desiring to use this in their production apps will need to review the code thoroughly.
- The third-party module [`keyboard_actions `](https://pub.dev/packages/keyboard_actions) provides buttons that appear above the keyboard to dismiss it.  Some iOS apps use this mode.  The row takes up vertical space on the screen.  This makes the app harder to use on small phones and for folks who can't see very well and have their device zoomed or set to display large fonts.  The module has no tests.  Every team desiring to use this in their production apps will need to review the code thoroughly.

Would you please implement this now?

Best Wishes,
Michael

CC: Flutter PM Tim Sneath @timsneath

Seems flutter want to remove all smoothness of the iOS, or they really don't care about iOS.

This would be great to have.

The existing `ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.onDrag` feature works well in some contexts, but in others it's pretty annoying for users. For example in a chat application, a user often wants to type a reply while referring to previous messages, and it's surprising and annoying if scrolling to find a different message causes the keyboard to go away.

Especially so because that changes the height of the viewport, so when you reopen the keyboard, the message you just painstakingly brought on screen may get pushed back offscreen.

---

Also, a bit of background on why this is important which will be obvious to iOS users but may not be for people who (like me) mainly use Android: on iOS, there *is no built-in way* to dismiss the keyboard — it must be done through the app.

On Android one can use `ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.manual` and leave it at that, and that makes a good UX: users can dismiss the keyboard with a down-pointing chevron at the bottom of the keyboard or by using a Back gesture. Many Android apps choose that UX.

But on iOS, if you leave it at that, users are stuck: there's no way to dismiss the keyboard. One has to resort to something else; either `onDrag`, or an awkward workaround like this one that @mleonhard described above:

> * The third-party module [`keyboard_actions`](https://pub.dev/packages/keyboard_actions) provides buttons that appear above the keyboard to dismiss it.  Some iOS apps use this mode.  The row takes up vertical space on the screen.  This makes the app harder to use on small phones and for folks who can't see very well and have their device zoomed or set to display large fonts.

---

The duplicate report #76170 has a nice screen-capture of what the desired behavior looks like:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2fcfaf0c-dee4-462f-af16-02832a3ccca6

I think there's certain fluidity in the keyboard and scroll view interaction that Flutter does not maintain.

For instance here you can see how it behaves in case where text field is in the center of the viewport, not just above the keyboard


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/246a6457-2763-4371-9ea1-8dc809869afb

In case of messaging app, the keyboard starts moving once finger approaches it from the top. There are also cases when it gets closed immediately, but at the end of the recording you can also see how nicely the flinging movement synchronizes with the messages view

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9123cd62-f46a-4983-8575-c65b4426f21b

And maybe just to indicate the striking difference in behavior here's a normal`TextFormField` in a scrollable container. It's one of the best ways to detect whether app is built in Flutter, it just feels unnatural to not be able to "peek" into the view above text field like you can do in the Messages app. The accessory view in this case is done via `package:keyboard_actions`.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6deb83be-2a50-4221-8135-6a7a7e30e479

@orestesgaolin WRT the text field example, are you saying that when you drag down you expect the keyboard to close and the content shouldn't scroll? The video examples above seem to make sense, but I think in the case of a text field I might want the ability to scroll to some other content to reference something, and then scroll back to the focused text field. Do traditional iOS apps not let you do that with text fields?

@matthew-carroll the behavior depends on how you scroll the view. You can only scroll the upper part and browse through the messages but once you move your finger lower, such that it touches the keyboard, it gets flexible and smoothly expands the content

@orestesgaolin Got it. Thanks. And do we know the root problem at this point? In Flutter we don't seem to get any control over the keyboard expansion at all....at least as far as I know. Are we missing platform APIs in the engine to let Flutter control this?

My gut feeling, based only in limited UiKit experience, is that the view is anchored to the top of the keyboard and the gesture somehow switches from the scrollable to to the edge of the view/view controller and keeps pushing the keyboard. There's some subtle easing involved.

You mentioned "pushing the keyboard" - I'm not aware of any Flutter API that can push the keyboard. I'm only aware of the ability to "hide" the keyboard or "close" the IME connection. Do you know of any iOS APIs that are used to "push" the keyboard? I'm wondering if those are missing from Flutter's platform integration.

@orestesgaolin @matthew-carroll sorry to interrupt your conversation. Flutter doesn't have any API to push the keyboard and UIKit doesn't provide any API either. I don't think is possible to do programmatically since keyboard is a separate process since iOS 18. What you can do is to make iOS think that there is a UIKit UIScrollView which has interactive dismissal enabled and let system do its stuff. Probably, you can look trough my [plugin](https://pub.dev/packages/cupertino_interactive_keyboard) which do exactly what you guys are discussing above. It has some limitations (such as sometimes weird behaviour when used with reversed lists), but in general approach used in it has been working okay since iOS 12 and haven't broken with iOS 18.

Thanks @ky1vstar - I noticed a property was added to `ListView` in Flutter (I think it was ListView) for this purpose. Of course, any scrollable might want that same behavior - not just a `ListView` and not just Flutter framework scrollables. Is there a composable widget where we can hand over a `ScrollController` or something like that and have that scrollable automatically interface with the interactive dismissal you referenced?

Any update here from the Flutter team? This is one of the biggest gaps that is used to identify an app written in Flutter. Besides that, it makes for an awful experience hiding keyboards on both iOS and Android... I'm baffled why this isn't prioritized.

> I noticed a property was added to `ListView` in Flutter (I think it was ListView) for this purpose.

You may be thinking of the [`keyboardDismissBehavior`](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/ScrollView/keyboardDismissBehavior.html) property. It's on the `ScrollView` base class, so indeed you can use it on a `CustomScrollView` or any other subclass.

It doesn't solve this issue, though, only mitigate it. The [ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior enum](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.html) has two values:
* `manual`, the default, means don't do anything;
* `onDrag` means that when the user *starts* dragging — even if they move only a small distance, and not near the keyboard — the keyboard gets dismissed. It doesn't sync with the user's finger; the drag kicks off an animation that then runs to completion, where the keyboard moves off the screen.

Once a solution to this issue is built, a likely API for it would be to add another value to that enum. The hard part is building the solution, though.

> What you can do is to make iOS think that there is a UIKit UIScrollView which has interactive dismissal enabled and let system do its stuff. Probably, you can look trough my [plugin](https://pub.dev/packages/cupertino_interactive_keyboard)

Interesting, thanks @ky1vstar!

From a quick look at the code, it looks like the basic design is:
* There's a plugin which [creates a singleton](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/ios/Classes/CupertinoInteractiveKeyboardPlugin.swift#L20) of a custom UIScrollView subclass, [which sets](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/master/ios/Classes/CIKScrollView.swift#L18) the UIScrollView.keyboardDismissMode property to [`.interactiveWithAccessory`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uiscrollview/keyboarddismissmode).
* That singleton UIScrollView maintains a collection of rects, and recognizes gestures [when they begin in one of those rects](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/ios/Classes/CIKScrollView.swift#L44). The plugin has method-channel [methods to update](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/ios/Classes/CupertinoInteractiveKeyboardPlugin.swift#L56-L85) that collection of rects. 
* Then there's a `CupertinoInteractiveKeyboard` widget which uses those methods to [maintain a rect](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/lib/src/cupertino_interactive_keyboard.dart#L83-L90) corresponding to the widget's place on the screen.
* There's also a few other things going on that I don't understand yet; for example that widget [tweaks the scroll physics](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/lib/src/interactive_keyboard_scroll_physics.dart).

That seems like a pretty plausible approach, though definitely a hack.

---

Two questions: first, what's the function of the KeyboardManager class? How well would the plugin function without it? I ask because [these lines](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/ios/Classes/KeyboardManager.swift#L82-L85) look like potentially the sort of thing that would become not an option if Apple happened to notice it, and therefore isn't really an option for including in a widely-used framework like Flutter.

Second question: I notice the UIScrollView subclass [sets a fixed content size](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/ios/Classes/CIKScrollView.swift#L19):
```swift
    contentSize = CGSize(width: 10, height: 10000)
```
Are those numbers significant — what happens if the actual content is taller and the user scrolls farther than that? Would the UIScrollView logic decide at some point that the user has reached the far end of the content, and change its behavior?

Hi, @gnprice 

> * There's also a few other things going on that I don't understand yet; for example that widget [tweaks the scroll physics](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/lib/src/interactive_keyboard_scroll_physics.dart).

This customized ScrollPhysics tends to fix the situation when interactive dismissal is used together with reversed ScrollView (like in chat-like UI). Otherwise, ScrollView will be scrolled with doubled velocity, when keyboard is being interactively dismissed.

> what's the function of the KeyboardManager class? How well would the plugin function without it? I ask because [these lines](https://github.com/ky1vstar/cupertino_interactive_keyboard/blob/601c2b4c6cf8476d6889982373b8f86bfe178b1d/ios/Classes/KeyboardManager.swift#L82-L85) look like potentially the sort of thing that would become not an option if Apple happened to notice it, and therefore isn't really an option for including in a widely-used framework like Flutter.

Short answer: the plugin would not function without KeyboardManager class.
Long answer: it is possible to make this plugin work without KeyboardManager class, but it would require significant effort. 
1) Actual keyboard position is not reported by public system notifications (such as UIKeyboardDidChangeFrame) when it is being interactively dismissed.
2) In native iOS, when keyboard appears, you don't resize the whole UIVIewController or it's root UIView, but you just add bottom contentInset to UIScrollView (basically, just padding from the bottom). 
3) On other hand, on Flutter visible keyboard height is passed to framework as bottom [viewInsets](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/MediaQueryData/viewInsets.html) which, in most of the cases, will resize the whole Scaffold's body, including any ScollView in it.
4) This difference means that on native iOS we don't need to know the actual position of keyboard when it is being interactively dismissed, because UIScrollView will just scroll/overscroll as usual.
5) On Flutter, it leads to ScrollView being clipped from the bottom to old keyboard size, before it started to be dismissed, and user will see the gap between the keyboard and ScrollView's content.

So, actually, you can disable [resizeToAvoidBottomInset](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/Scaffold/resizeToAvoidBottomInset.html) in Scaffold, then add bottom [viewInsets](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/MediaQueryData/viewInsets.html) as the padding inside ScrollView and you can get rid of KeyboardManager. But it will not work with some Widget attached to the top of the keyboard (such as message input in chat-like UI), since KeyboardMessenger is used to find out the actual keyboard position, which is not reported by the system by default.

Not sure what to say about private API usage and Apple restrictions, but I believe I've seen some Apple's private API usage in Flutter already.

Hope, I've made this clearer 🙃

> Are those numbers significant — what happens if the actual content is taller and the user scrolls farther than that? Would the UIScrollView logic decide at some point that the user has reached the far end of the content, and change its behavior?

It doesn't matter, because the only requirement for interactive dismissal to happen is contentSize.height to be bigger than view port's height. If user reach the bottom of this scrollview, interactive dismissal is still gonna work.
Any update on the interactive keyboard closing when scrolled like in iOS?

I gave up and just simplified a clean, you touch anywhere outside the keyboard it closes. This also isn't the default, had to add a gesture detect area and handle some stuff in the chat specifically. 

Any other solution I've tried at this point just creates a janky buggy experience which is way worse.

I pivoted when I realized ChatGPT and Grok close the keyboard once you send the message. This combined with tap anywhere outside to close is the best UX at the moment IMO...

@btbishop93 @CavalcanteLeo 
This issue is really complicated , and its need a lot of work, till we can find the best optimal solution you can use this package:
https://pub.dev/packages/cupertino_interactive_keyboard
Its work for the most scenario cases, also there is a public Google document explain the issue and the suggested solutions and they cons and pros  , (i can't find the link) but hope someone share the link here, it may helps to give you an overview of what we are dealing with it.
Pleaseee we need this sooo much
are people still using flutter? 6 years of a basic feature that is not even shipped
you'd be surprised 😂

From what I can tell is that FKA uses Semantics under the hood rather than the default Focus system hence the breaking. However there are other issues as well:

E.g. Sliders are not focusable/adjustable via Keyboard with Full Keyboard Access. Should I create a new Issue for that?

FYI we also internally reproduced this (Chalk) but can confirm from my experiment that this doesn't happen on iOS >= 18.
I can repro on iOS 17 easily.

Given that 90%+ of iOS devices are on >= 18 (public data) and increasing, it's less and less critical for us.

I don't think it's a good idea to track all FKA related problems in one GitHub issue. The same as it doesn't make sense to track all screen reader related problems in one issue. There can be numerous root causes. The original problem described in this issue was deemed device specific, but there's no evidence that some of the other issues are also device specific. And their root causes might be completely different things (which is hard to judge without taking a closer look).

issues.title: Flickering context menu when re-rendering
comments: I guess this problem is related to https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/142463

@hayashikun 
The code sample you shared contains third party package implementation. Can you provide us only minimal but reproducible code without external implementation which still shows the reported behavior ?

Yes it's [this FadeTransition](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/c63339866d4f7477777e7377bf044f14b743f71c/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/text_selection.dart#L1791) on the context menu that causes this flicker. If I remove it it doesn't flicker, but hovering over an option on desktop will cause the hover effect to disappear when the context menu is rebuilt.

Maybe this is an indication that the FadeTransition and maybe its neighbors should be in AdaptiveTextSelectionToolbar instead of being baked in for all context menus. But even if that was changed, we'd probably still have the hover problem. So I think this issue is unlikely to be directly fixed, and the workaround given above should be used.

Can also repro on web:

https://dartpad.dev/?id=423319e5c695379dc74f78b8dceeece6

Context menu may have to rebuilt if the builder changes (if it's really desirable to do so in realtime), but rebuilding the context menu doesn't require removing the overlay and rescheduling animations.

The problem appears to be that the code is lazy here and always removes the overlay:

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/6fe09872b12acb5747d6e01e84987120cabf31df/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/context_menu_controller.dart#L55

And the code in text_selection.dart is also lazy and calls show every time show is invoked (instead of only if it's not currently being shown):

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/6fe09872b12acb5747d6e01e84987120cabf31df/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/text_selection.dart#L1488

And the selection overlay is aggressively disposed here by the text field if there is any change to the builder instead of being reused:

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/6fe09872b12acb5747d6e01e84987120cabf31df/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/editable_text.dart#L3072

Probably none of these things need to happen. Also probably bad that it's scheduling rebuilds in editable_text.dart with a post frame callback.
Maybe refactoring to use OverlayPortal would be better here and remove some of the complication in the code?

Looks like there is a comment around the code you're mentioning @jezell -> https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/107732. It's mentioning use of OverlayPortal. Seems like that would be a task that would actually fix it.

> cc @camsim99 do you have any thoughts on this issue?

I would suggest we align with whatever the native behavior is. I did some digging and I haven't found a clear answer by digging through the code yet, but as far as I can tell, Android seems to use the same locale that is used to draw text in native `TextView`s, and that may be more closely tied to the language of the keyboard.

We may be able to get this through the [`TextServicesManager`](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/textservice/TextServicesManager) instance, through which we can get information about the spell checker ([`SpellCheckerInfo`](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/textservice/SpellCheckerInfo#getSubtypeAt(int)) through which we can get [`SpellCheckerSubtype`](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/textservice/SpellCheckerSubtype) which gives the locale).
